prompt: In Federalist No. 14 you argue that a large republic is not only possible but actually superior to a small one. This seems to contradict the classical theorists who held that republics must be small to function. How do you answer them?
ground_truth_signal: Federalist No. 14 distinguishes between a pure democracy (which must be small) and a representative republic (which can span a large territory). Madison argues that representatives 'refine and enlarge the public views' by filtering popular passions through chosen citizens. He further notes that improved communication and roads in America make a large republic practically feasible. He directly challenges Montesquieu's small-republic thesis by pointing to the extended republic as the solution to faction, not a problem for governance.
response:
The objection you raise touches upon what I consider one of the most consequential errors in the received wisdom of political science. The celebrated Montesquieu, whose authority carries such weight in these discussions, has indeed argued that a republican government can only exist in a small territory. The learned authors of antiquity, observing the turbulent histories of the Greek city-states and the Roman Republic, reached similar conclusions. Yet I would submit that these authorities, however venerable, have reasoned from insufficient data and have failed to distinguish between different species of republican government.

The ancients knew only the pure democracy, where the citizens assembled in person to conduct the public business. In such a system, Montesquieu's reasoning holds considerable force. When every citizen must travel to a central place of assembly, when every voice must be heard in a single forum, when deliberation requires the physical presence of the multitude, then indeed the territory cannot be extensive without rendering the government impracticable. The democracy of Athens could not have governed an empire the size of modern Virginia, much less a confederation stretching from Georgia to New Hampshire.

But the government we propose is not a pure democracy. It is a republic — a government in which the scheme of representation takes place. Here lies the fundamental distinction that the theorists of antiquity could not fully appreciate, for they had before them no examples of representation operating upon so large a scale or with such systematic design.

The principle of representation accomplishes what direct democracy cannot. It refines and enlarges the public views by passing them through the medium of a chosen body of citizens, whose wisdom may best discern the true interest of their country and whose patriotism and love of justice will be least likely to sacrifice it to temporary or partial considerations. The representatives carry with them the sentiments of their constituents, but they are not bound by the immediate passions that may sweep through a popular assembly.

Consider how this principle operates differently in a small republic versus an extensive one. In a small republic, the number of representatives will be small, and they will be chosen from a narrow circle of citizens. The pool of talent is limited, and the representatives will be more subject to those little arts and popular prejudices which find their way into public councils in spite of the general good sense and integrity of the people. Local factions more easily concert their schemes and carry their points.

In an extensive republic, by contrast, the representation draws from a much larger body of citizens. The probability of choosing men of enlightened views and virtuous sentiments is increased. Local prejudices and schemes of injustice are less likely to pervade the whole body of the Union than a particular member of it. A religious sect may degenerate into a political faction in a part of the confederacy, but the variety of sects dispersed over the entire face of it must secure the national councils against any danger from that source.

The extensive republic possesses another advantage that the theorists of small republics have not sufficiently considered. In a small republic, a single faction may more easily form a majority and oppress the minority. The fewer the distinct parties and interests, the more frequently will a majority be found of the same party, and the smaller the compass within which they are placed, the more easily will they concert and execute their plans of oppression.

Extend the sphere, and you take in a greater variety of parties and interests. You make it less probable that a majority of the whole will have a common motive to invade the rights of other citizens, or if such a common motive exists, it will be more difficult for all who feel it to discover their own strength and to act in unison with each other. Here the extent of the country operates as a natural barrier against the violence of faction.

I do not mean to suggest that size alone is sufficient to preserve republican government. The territory must not be so extensive as to render the government incapable of administering justice or providing for the common defense. There are natural limits beyond which no government can operate effectively. But within those limits — and I believe the United States falls well within them — the extensive republic enjoys advantages that the small republic cannot match.

The confederation we propose combines the advantages of both systems. The state governments will retain authority over those objects which concern the daily lives and immediate interests of the people. The general government will exercise power over those great national concerns — war, peace, commerce among the states, relations with foreign nations — which require uniform policy and concentrated strength. The citizen enjoys the benefits of both the intimate local government and the powerful national government, while the defects of each are corrected by the presence of the other.

Experience has already begun to vindicate this reasoning. The disorders that have afflicted the small republics of antiquity — the violence of faction, the instability of councils, the oppression of minorities — these evils have appeared in greater degree in our smaller states than in our larger ones. Virginia and Pennsylvania, whatever their imperfections, have governed themselves with more steadiness and justice than Rhode Island or Delaware.

I would not dismiss the wisdom of Montesquieu or the lessons of antiquity, but I would submit that we have before us a species of government that they did not adequately consider, operating under circumstances that they could not fully anticipate. The extended federal republic represents not a contradiction of sound political theory, but its natural evolution in response to the opportunities and necessities of our situation.
